page: Hetzner Cloud cpx52 is an x86_64 virtual server powered by the QEMU hypervisor. It features 12 shared vCPUs based on the AMD EPYC Genoa architecture running at 2.0 GHz. The hardware configuration includes 23.0 GB of system memory, providing approximately 1.95 GB of RAM per core. Local storage is bundled with 480 GB of SSD capacity. There are no GPUs or complimentary public IPv4 addresses included with this instance.

Benchmark data indicates strong single-core performance, with a stress-ng single-core CPU score of 3041.68 and a Geekbench single-core score of 1947.0, both placing in the top 25% of tested hardware. Memory performance is also strong for large-block operations, achieving 220.5 GB/sec read and 143.81 GB/sec write speeds. Multi-core performance is average, with a Geekbench multi-core score of 10098.0 and a Passmark CPU score of 28868.84. Small-model LLM inference is highly efficient, reaching 2446.6 tokens/sec for prompt processing and 304.2 tokens/sec for text generation.

The cpx52 offers a cost-efficient profile for compute-heavy workloads due to its high core count relative to its resource class, though the shared CPU allocation represents a potential tradeoff for sustained multi-core performance consistency. It is well-suited for workloads such as software compilation, text processing, web serving, and small-to-medium LLM inference tasks where single-core bursts and memory bandwidth are prioritized over dedicated CPU guarantees.
description: Hetzner Cloud cpx52 is an x86_64 virtual server running on the QEMU hypervisor, featuring 12 shared AMD EPYC Genoa vCPUs clocked at 2.0 GHz. It is equipped with 23.0 GB of RAM and 480 GB of local SSD storage, with no integrated GPUs or complimentary public IPv4 addresses. Benchmarks show strong single-core capabilities, with a Geekbench single-core score of 1947.0, alongside strong large-block memory read and write bandwidth. Multi-core performance and database operations sit in the average tier. Because the CPU resources are shared, the cpx52 represents a budget-friendly option for compute-focused workloads that do not require dedicated cores. It is best suited for tasks such as software compilation, web serving, and small-scale LLM inference.
